function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

**FY2025 Space Segment Revenue**

    *   *Launch & Development Revenue:* $1,510 million

**Mass-to-Orbit KPIs (FY2025)**
*   **Total Mass to Orbit:** 2,213 metric tons 
    *   *Customer Payloads:* 312 metric tons
    *   *Internal Payloads:* 1,901 metric tons
*   **Global Share:** The 2,200+ metric tons launched represented over 80% of mass to orbit for the world in 2025.
*   **Historical Total:** As of March 31, 2026, SpaceX had launched a total mass to orbit of approximately 7,400 metric tons historically.
*   **Falcon 9 Capacity:** Falcon 9 launches contribute steadily at an average capacity of 13 metric tons per mission since 2023.

**Launch Volume / Number of Launches (FY2025)**
*   **Total Missions:** 170 missions across Falcon and Starship vehicles.
*   **Total Falcon Launches:** 165 launches 
    *   *Customer Launches:* 43
    *   *Internal Launches:* 122
*   **Flight-Proven Booster Launches:** 159 flight-proven booster launches with an over 99% success rate on attempted booster recoveries.
*   **Historical Total:** Approximately 650 orbital space launches completed (as of March 31, 2026), with over 540 of those completed by a flight-proven Falcon rocket.

I have the key data I need. Let me now calculate the implied revenue-per-kilogram proxy.

FY2025 Space segment revenue: $4,086 million = $4,086,000,000
Total mass to orbit FY2025: 2,213 metric tons = 2,213,000 kg

Implied revenue per kg = $4,086,000,000 / 2,213,000 kg = $1,846.37 per kg

But wait - this is a proxy that mixes internal Starlink launches (which don't generate external revenue) with customer launches. Let me think about this more carefully.

The total mass to orbit includes:
- Customer payloads: 312 metric tons
- Internal payloads (Starlink): 1,901 metric tons

If we use only customer payloads (312 metric tons = 312,000 kg):
Revenue per kg = $4,086,000,000 / 312,000 kg = $13,096.15 per kg

But this is also problematic because the $4,086M includes both Launch Services ($2,576M) and Launch & Development ($1,510M). The Launch & Development revenue includes development contracts (like NASA HLS, DoD development work) that aren't tied to mass-to-orbit.

Let me think about the most appropriate proxy:

Option 1: Total Space Revenue / Total Mass to Orbit
= $4,086M / 2,213 metric tons
= $4,086,000,000 / 2,213,000 kg
= $1,846.37 per kg

Option 2: Launch Services Revenue / Customer Payload Mass
= $2,576M / 312 metric tons
= $2,576,000,000 / 312,000 kg
= $8,256.41 per kg

Option 3: Total Space Revenue / Customer Payload Mass
= $4,086M / 312 metric tons
= $4,086,000,000 / 312,000 kg
= $13,096.15 per kg

Option 4: Launch Services Revenue / Total Mass to Orbit
= $2,576M / 2,213 metric tons
= $2,576,000,000 / 2,213,000 kg
= $1,164.26 per kg

The most appropriate proxy for comparison to legacy launch providers (which charge per kg to LEO for customer payloads) would be Option 2: Launch Services Revenue / Customer Payload Mass. This is because:
- Launch Services revenue is the externally-billed launch revenue
- Customer payload mass is the externally-launched mass
- Legacy providers' list prices are for customer payloads to LEO

But Option 1 (Total Space Revenue / Total Mass to Orbit) is also a useful directional proxy because it captures SpaceX's overall economics including internal Starlink launches.

Let me also consider that the filing mentions historical cost per kilogram figures:
- Historical average launch cost: ~$18,500 per kilogram
- Falcon 9 (2010): ~$2,700 per kilogram
- Falcon Heavy (2018): $1,400 per kilogram

These are SpaceX's own historical cost-per-kilogram figures, which are useful context.

Now let me compare to legacy launch providers:

ULA:
- Atlas V: ~$153 million per launch, LEO capacity ~18,850 kg → ~$8,117/kg
- Vulcan: ~$100 million per launch (target), LEO capacity ~27,200 kg → ~$3,676/kg (target)

Arianespace:
- Ariane 6 (Ariane 64): ~$90 million per launch, LEO capacity ~21,500 kg → ~$4,186/kg
- Ariane 5: was around $10,000/kg

Let me search for more specific data on these.

Actually, let me also note that the question asks about "list prices per kilogram to LEO" - so I should focus on the publicly known list prices.

Let me search for more specific data on ULA and Arianespace list prices per kg to LEO.

Based on the provided text, here are the relevant disclosures regarding the Space segment's accounting treatment, cost of revenue drivers, per-kilogram economics, and profitability metrics:

### Accounting Treatment for Reusable vs. Non-Reusable Vehicle Components
*   **Reusable Components:** "Boosters, fairings, and Merlin engines are reusable and are classified as property, plant, and equipment and are depreciated to cost of revenue." Additionally, "Dragon is comprised of a fully reusable capsule that is classified as Property, plant, and equipment, net and is depreciated to cost of revenue."
*   **Non-Reusable Components:** "The second stages are not reusable and are recorded to cost of revenue when they are launched for Launch Services revenue transactions or assigned for Launch and Development revenue transactions."
*   **Starship (In Development):** "Starship is comprised of a booster, ship, and Raptor engines and is currently in the development stage. A majority of Starship costs are currently expensed to Research and development as incurred. Raptor engines are expensed when used in test flights." However, "At commercialization, Starship costs generally will be capitalized and then depreciated in cost of revenue of the segment associated with the payload delivered."

### Space Segment Cost of Revenue Drivers and Future Trends
*   **Cost of Revenue Drivers:** "Space segment’s cost of revenue includes second stages flown related to the Company’s Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy launches, launch operations and overhead, depreciation (inclusive of booster, Merlin engine, and fairing depreciation), employee compensation costs (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation) for our operations teams, launch testing and overhead, engineering costs, inventory excess and obsolescence, shared costs incurred in the production of launch hardware, and ongoing product support."
*   **Future Trends:** "We expect Space cost of revenue to increase both in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue based on our expected mix of Launch Services and Launch and Development. From period to period, Space segment cost of revenue will vary based on the mix of customer and internal launches."

### Per-Kilogram Economics and Historical Cost Reductions
*   **Historical Average:** The text cites a historical average launch cost of **$18,500 per kilogram**.
*   **Falcon 9:** "According to NASA, the first version of Falcon 9 in 2010 reduced launch cost to approximately **$2,700 per kilogram**, which represented a reduction of approximately **85%** compared to the historical average launch cost per kilogram of $18,500."
*   **Falcon Heavy:** "The first version of Falcon Heavy in 2018 further reduced this cost to **$1,400 per kilogram**, a reduction of approximately **92%** compared to the historical average."
*   **Future Starship Goals:** "With the future deployment of Starship, which is designed to be the world’s first fully and rapidly reusable spacecraft, we aim to further reduce the cost to reach orbit by **99% or more** relative to the historical average launch cost."
*   **Reusability Impact:** "Partial reusability for orbital spaceflight has reduced cost per ton to orbit by approximately 85% as compared to the historical average launch cost per kilogram of $18,500."

### Space Segment Profitability and Cost of Revenue Metrics
*   **Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** 
    *   **2025:** $653 million (a decrease of $501 million compared to $1,154 million in 2024). The decrease was "primarily driven by an accelerated investment in development of the Starship vehicle, as well as launch facilities to support future Starship launches, partially offset by an increase in NASA Cargo Resupply Services (CRS)... along with increased revenue from a U.S. Department of War contract."
    *   **2024:** $1,154 million (an increase of $157 million compared to $997 million in 2023).
    *   **Q1 2026:** $(351) million, compared to $224 million in Q1 2025.
*   **Segment Income (Loss) from Operations:**
    *   **Q1 2026:** $(662) million
    *   **Q1 2025:** $(70) million
    *   **2025:** $(657) million
    *   **2024:** $21 million
    *   **2023:** $(1) million
*   **Year-over-Year Cost of Revenue Changes:**
    *   **FY 2025:** Decreased by $189 million (12.2%) compared to 2024. "This decrease was primarily due increased reusability of our Falcon launch vehicles resulting in lower deprecation of $240 million, lowering the cost of each launch, and lower overhead costs of $11 million." (Partially offset by a $51M increase in inventory excess/obsolescence reserves).
    *   **FY 2024:** Decreased by $128 million (7.6%) compared to 2023. "This decrease was primarily due to increased reusability of our Falcon launch vehicles resulting in lower depreciation of $80 million, lowering the cost of each launch."
    *   **Q1 2026:** Decreased by $16 million compared to Q1 2025 "due to less customer launches."

arguments.final_result:
## SpaceX S-1: Implied Revenue-per-Kilogram Proxy vs. Legacy Launch Providers

### 1. Disclosed SpaceX FY2025 Space Segment Inputs (from S-1)

**Revenue (recast consolidated entity):**
| Component | FY2025 ($M) |
|---|---|
| Launch Services (point-in-time) | $2,576 |
| Launch & Development (over-time) | $1,510 |
| **Total Space segment revenue** | **$4,086** |

**Mass-to-orbit KPI (FY2025):**
| Metric | FY2025 |
|---|---|
| Total mass to orbit | 2,213 metric tons |
| — Customer payloads | 312 metric tons |
| — Internal (Starlink) payloads | 1,901 metric tons |
| Total launches (Falcon + Starship) | 170 |
| Falcon launches (43 customer / 122 internal) | 165 |
| Avg. Falcon 9 capacity/mission (since 2023) | ~13 metric tons |

SpaceX also discloses **historical cost-per-kilogram** benchmarks (these are *cost*, not price):
- Historical industry average: ~$18,500/kg
- Falcon 9 (2010): ~$2,700/kg (–85% vs. historical)
- Falcon Heavy (2018): ~$1,400/kg (–92% vs. historical)

### 2. Implied Revenue-per-Kilogram Proxies

Because the S-1 does not publish a revenue-per-kg figure, four directional proxies can be constructed from disclosed inputs:

| Proxy | Calculation | $/kg |
|---|---|---|
| A. Total Space Rev ÷ Total Mass to Orbit | $4,086M ÷ 2,213 t | **$1,846** |
| B. Launch Services Rev ÷ Customer Payload Mass | $2,576M ÷ 312 t | **$8,256** |
| C. Total Space Rev ÷ Customer Payload Mass | $4,086M ÷ 312 t | **$13,096** |
| D. Launch Services Rev ÷ Total Mass to Orbit | $2,576M ÷ 2,213 t | **$1,164** |

**Proxy B ($8,256/kg) is the most apples-to-apples comparison** to legacy list prices, because it isolates externally-billed launch revenue (Launch Services) against externally-deployed customer mass — the same numerator/denominator concept legacy providers quote. Proxy A is useful as a blended economic-intensity metric but is depressed by ~86% of mass being internal Starlink deployments that generate no third-party launch revenue.

### 3. Legacy Launch Provider List Prices (publicly available)

| Provider / Vehicle | List Price | LEO Capacity | Implied $/kg to LEO |
|---|---|---|---|
| ULA Atlas V 551 | ~$153M | ~18,850 kg | **~$8,117** |
| ULA Vulcan Centaur (6-solid) | ~$110M | ~27,200 kg | **~$4,044** |
| Arianespace Ariane 5 (legacy) | ~€150–200M | ~20,000 kg | **~$8,900** |
| Arianespace Ariane 64 | ~$77–115M (≈$90M Amazon Leo) | ~21,650 kg | **~$4,157** (range ~$3,557–$5,312) |

### 4. Directional Comparison & Interpretation

**Pricing power:** SpaceX's customer-facing implied revenue per kg (~$8,256/kg via Proxy B) is *comparable to or modestly above* legacy list prices for Atlas V (~$8,117/kg) and Ariane 5 (~$8,900/kg), and *well above* next-generation expendable vehicles like Vulcan (~$4,044/kg) and Ariane 64 (~$4,157/kg). This is striking because SpaceX is widely perceived as the low-cost disruptor — yet on a customer-revenue-per-kg basis it is pricing at or near legacy levels. The implication is that SpaceX is **not passing through the full extent of its cost advantage to customers**; instead it is capturing a substantial portion of the cost gap as margin, consistent with significant pricing power driven by dominant market share (>80% of global mass-to-orbit in 2025 per the S-1) and limited competitive alternatives at scale.

**Margin structure:** The S-1's own historical cost benchmarks ($1,400–$2,700/kg for Falcon-class vehicles) versus the ~$8,256/kg customer revenue proxy imply a very wide gross spread per kilogram — potentially $5,000–$7,000/kg of contribution before overhead — for externally-billed launches. This is consistent with the structural advantage of partial reusability (booster/fairing/Merlin depreciation rather than full expendable cost), which the S-1 explicitly credits with lowering Space segment cost of revenue by $240M of depreciation in FY2025 alone.

**Caveat on reported segment profitability:** Despite the favorable per-kg economics, FY2025 Space segment **Income from Operations was a loss of $(657)M** (–16.08% margin) and **Segment Adjusted EBITDA fell to $653M** (15.98% margin, down from $1,154M in 2024). The S-1 attributes this to accelerated Starship development and launch-facility investment. So the per-kg margin proxy reflects *Falcon-era* economics, while the reported segment P&L is dragged by Starship R&D and capital build-out that is not yet generating commercial mass-to-orbit revenue.

### 5. Filing Disclosure Gap Preventing a Precise Margin-per-Kilogram Calculation

The S-1 does **not** disclose the inputs required to compute a precise margin-per-kilogram. Specifically, the following are absent:

1. **No cost-of-revenue split between customer and internal launches.** The S-1 discloses aggregate Space segment cost of revenue drivers (second stages, depreciation, operations overhead, etc.) but does not allocate cost between Launch Services (customer) and Launch & Development, nor between customer and internal Starlink missions. Internal Starlink launches consume second stages, range, and operations resources but generate no third-party launch revenue, so they materially distort any blended revenue-per-kg or margin-per-kg calculation.

2. **No current-period cost-per-kilogram figure.** The only per-kg cost disclosures are *historical* benchmarks ($18,500 historical average; $2,700 for Falcon 9 v1.0 in 2010; $1,400 for Falcon Heavy in 2018). There is no FY2025 cost-per-kg, so a current margin-per-kg cannot be derived from filing data alone.

3. **No per-launch revenue or per-launch cost.** The S-1 reports aggregate Launch Services revenue ($2,576M) and aggregate customer launches (43) but does not disclose average revenue per customer launch, nor does it disclose cost per launch. A per-launch margin cannot be computed.

4. **No gross margin or segment profit attributable specifically to customer launch services.** Segment Adjusted EBITDA and Income from Operations are reported at the total Space segment level, which commingles (a) customer Launch Services, (b) Launch & Development (e.g., NASA HLS, DoD development, CRS), and (c) Starship R&D. There is no sub-segment profitability disclosure that would isolate the customer launch margin pool.

5. **No orbit-type or mission-type breakdown.** Legacy list prices are quoted to LEO; SpaceX's customer payload mass (312 t) and Launch Services revenue ($2,576M) include GEO/GTO and other non-LEO missions, which command different per-kg economics. The S-1 does not disaggregate revenue or mass by destination orbit.

Because of these gaps, the $8,256/kg figure (Proxy B) should be treated as a **directional revenue-intensity proxy**, not a realized price-per-kg, and no filing-supported margin-per-kg can be computed. The directional comparison nonetheless supports the conclusion that SpaceX retains substantial pricing power relative to legacy expendable providers and that its cost structure (reusability-driven) affords a wide per-kg gross spread that is currently being reinvested into Starship rather than reflected in segment operating income.

## SpaceX S-1: Implied Revenue-per-Kilogram Proxy vs. Legacy Launch Providers

### 1. Disclosed SpaceX FY2025 Space Segment Inputs (from S-1)

**Revenue (recast consolidated entity):**
| Component | FY2025 ($M) |
|---|---|
| Launch Services (point-in-time) | $2,576 |
| Launch & Development (over-time) | $1,510 |
| **Total Space segment revenue** | **$4,086** |

**Mass-to-orbit KPI (FY2025):**
| Metric | FY2025 |
|---|---|
| Total mass to orbit | 2,213 metric tons |
| — Customer payloads | 312 metric tons |
| — Internal (Starlink) payloads | 1,901 metric tons |
| Total launches (Falcon + Starship) | 170 |
| Falcon launches (43 customer / 122 internal) | 165 |
| Avg. Falcon 9 capacity/mission (since 2023) | ~13 metric tons |

SpaceX also discloses **historical cost-per-kilogram** benchmarks (these are *cost*, not price):
- Historical industry average: ~$18,500/kg
- Falcon 9 (2010): ~$2,700/kg (–85% vs. historical)
- Falcon Heavy (2018): ~$1,400/kg (–92% vs. historical)

### 2. Implied Revenue-per-Kilogram Proxies

Because the S-1 does not publish a revenue-per-kg figure, four directional proxies can be constructed from disclosed inputs:

| Proxy | Calculation | $/kg |
|---|---|---|
| A. Total Space Rev ÷ Total Mass to Orbit | $4,086M ÷ 2,213 t | **$1,846** |
| B. Launch Services Rev ÷ Customer Payload Mass | $2,576M ÷ 312 t | **$8,256** |
| C. Total Space Rev ÷ Customer Payload Mass | $4,086M ÷ 312 t | **$13,096** |
| D. Launch Services Rev ÷ Total Mass to Orbit | $2,576M ÷ 2,213 t | **$1,164** |

**Proxy B ($8,256/kg) is the most apples-to-apples comparison** to legacy list prices, because it isolates externally-billed launch revenue (Launch Services) against externally-deployed customer mass — the same numerator/denominator concept legacy providers quote. Proxy A is useful as a blended economic-intensity metric but is depressed by ~86% of mass being internal Starlink deployments that generate no third-party launch revenue.

### 3. Legacy Launch Provider List Prices (publicly available)

| Provider / Vehicle | List Price | LEO Capacity | Implied $/kg to LEO |
|---|---|---|---|
| ULA Atlas V 551 | ~$153M | ~18,850 kg | **~$8,117** |
| ULA Vulcan Centaur (6-solid) | ~$110M | ~27,200 kg | **~$4,044** |
| Arianespace Ariane 5 (legacy) | ~€150–200M | ~20,000 kg | **~$8,900** |
| Arianespace Ariane 64 | ~$77–115M (≈$90M Amazon Leo) | ~21,650 kg | **~$4,157** (range ~$3,557–$5,312) |

This is striking because SpaceX is widely perceived as the low-cost disruptor — yet on a customer-revenue-per-kg basis it is pricing at or near legacy levels. The implication is that SpaceX is **not passing through the full extent of its cost advantage to customers**; instead it is capturing a substantial portion of the cost gap as margin, consistent with significant pricing power driven by dominant market share (>80% of global mass-to-orbit in 2025 per the S-1) and limited competitive alternatives at scale.

**Margin structure:** The S-1's own historical cost benchmarks ($1,400–$2,700/kg for Falcon-class vehicles) versus the ~$8,256/kg customer revenue proxy imply a very wide gross spread per kilogram — potentially $5,000–$7,000/kg of contribution before overhead — for externally-billed launches. This is consistent with the structural advantage of partial reusability (booster/fairing/Merlin depreciation rather than full expendable cost), which the S-1 explicitly credits with lowering Space segment cost of revenue by $240M of depreciation in FY2025 alone.

**Caveat on reported segment profitability:** Despite the favorable per-kg economics, FY2025 Space segment **Income from Operations was a loss of $(657)M** (–16.08% margin) and **Segment Adjusted EBITDA fell to $653M** (15.98% margin, down from $1,154M in 2024). The S-1 attributes this to accelerated Starship development and launch-facility investment. So the per-kg margin proxy reflects *Falcon-era* economics, while the reported segment P&L is dragged by Starship R&D and capital build-out that is not yet generating commercial mass-to-orbit revenue.
